questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to view all existing users in the account along with their default roles and the date they last logged in. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS;
SHOW USERS;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USERS;
DESCRIBE ALL USERS;

questions.explanation: SHOW USERS displays all users with their properties including login_name, display_name, default_role, default_warehouse, last_success_login, and other metadata. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS is also valid and provides historical/queryable data with SQL, but SHOW USERS is the direct DDL command that provides this information. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not have a USERS view. DESCRIBE ALL USERS is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: User Management: SHOW USERS

questions.stem: A company's security policy requires that all Snowflake passwords be at least 16 characters, contain at least 2 uppercase letters, 2 lowercase letters, 2 numbers, and 2 special characters, and not match any of the last 5 passwords. Which CREATE PASSWORD POLICY statement implements these requirements?

options.text: CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH = 16 PASSWORD_MIN_UPPER_CASE_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_LOWER_CASE_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_NUMERIC_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_SPECIAL_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_HISTORY = 5;
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy MIN_LENGTH = 16 COMPLEXITY = (UPPER=2, LOWER=2, NUMERIC=2, SPECIAL=2) HISTORY = 5;
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy WITH (MIN_LENGTH = 16, MIN_UPPERCASE = 2, MIN_LOWERCASE = 2, MIN_DIGITS = 2, MIN_SPECIAL = 2, MAX_REUSE = 5);
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy ENFORCE MIN_LENGTH 16 COMPLEXITY UPPER 2 LOWER 2 NUMERIC 2 SPECIAL 2 NO_REUSE 5;

questions.explanation: The correct Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY parameters use the full prefixed names: PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH, PASSWORD_MIN_UPPER_CASE_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_LOWER_CASE_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_NUMERIC_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_SPECIAL_CHARS, and PASSWORD_HISTORY. Options B, C, and D use incorrect parameter names that are not valid in Snowflake's PASSWORD POLICY syntax.
questions.reference: Password Policies: CREATE PASSWORD POLICY Parameters

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is auditing the account's role hierarchy and discovers a custom role FINANCE_ADMIN that was created but never added to the standard hierarchy. Select TWO consequences of this configuration gap.

options.text: Objects created by roles under FINANCE_ADMIN are not visible to SYSADMIN through normal role hierarchy inheritance
Users cannot be granted the FINANCE_ADMIN role until it is added to the hierarchy
ACCOUNTADMIN cannot view or manage objects owned by FINANCE_ADMIN
Only ACCOUNTADMIN can manage users and objects associated with FINANCE_ADMIN because it is outside the normal hierarchy

questions.explanation: When a custom role is not granted to SYSADMIN, objects owned by that role (or roles beneath it) are not accessible to SYSADMIN through hierarchy inheritance. SYSADMIN cannot manage, drop, or modify those objects. However, ACCOUNTADMIN inherits ALL roles including orphaned ones, so ACCOUNTADMIN retains visibility and management capability (option D is correct). Option B is false — users can still be granted any role regardless of hierarchy position. Option C is false — ACCOUNTADMIN always has full access.
questions.reference: Role Hierarchy: Orphaned Custom Roles

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is on Enterprise edition. An administrator sets the account-level MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. A table owner sets DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 90 on a specific table. What is the effective Time Travel retention for that table?

options.text: 14 days, because MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS caps the retention at 14 days
90 days, because the table-level setting overrides the account-level MAX setting
90 days, because MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls Fail-Safe extension, not Time Travel retention
7 days, which is the Enterprise edition maximum for Time Travel

questions.explanation: MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls how long Snowflake can extend data retention beyond the set TIME_TRAVEL period when needed by Streams consuming the table — it is not a cap on DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS itself. DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table is independently set to 90 days and that is the effective Time Travel retention. On Enterprise edition, the maximum Time Travel retention is 90 days. Option A misunderstands what MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS does. Option D is wrong — Enterprise supports up to 90 days.
questions.reference: Time Travel: DATA_RETENTION vs MAX_DATA_EXTENSION

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake at the Organization level with multiple accounts (PROD, DEV, STAGING). An administrator needs to move a database from the DEV account to the PROD account. Which approach is supported natively in Snowflake?

options.text: Use ALTER DATABASE dev_db TRANSFER TO ACCOUNT prod_account;
Use database replication (CREATE DATABASE prod_db AS REPLICA OF dev_account.dev_db) to create a replica in PROD, then promote it
Use the ORGADMIN role to run MOVE DATABASE dev_db TO ACCOUNT prod_account;
Export all tables to an external stage, then import into the PROD account

questions.explanation: Snowflake's database replication and failover feature allows creating a replica of a database in another account within the same organization. The workflow is: enable replication on the source account, create a replicated database in the target account, refresh the replica, and (optionally) promote it to a primary. ALTER DATABASE TRANSFER and MOVE DATABASE are not valid Snowflake commands. The external stage export/import (option D) works but is not a native first-class database move feature — it's a manual data migration. Database replication is the intended native approach.
questions.reference: Account Administration: Database Replication Across Accounts

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to configure the account so that all users are required to authenticate using SSO (SAML 2.0) through their corporate identity provider, with no password-based login allowed. Which steps are required?

options.text: Configure a SAML integration, set the default_login to SSO, and run ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE
Create a SECURITY INTEGRATION of type SAML2, configure the IdP, and optionally disable native password auth via a LOGIN POLICY with ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE
Run CREATE SAML PROVIDER, then run ALTER ALL USERS SET AUTHENTICATION = SSO_ONLY
Enable SSO in the Snowflake UI settings and set REQUIRE_SSO = TRUE in a session policy

questions.explanation: SSO via SAML2 in Snowflake requires creating a SECURITY INTEGRATION (CREATE SECURITY INTEGRATION ... TYPE = SAML2) configured with the identity provider's metadata. To prevent password-based logins, a LOGIN POLICY can be configured with ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE and applied at the account or user level. The exact parameter name may vary — the key concepts are: SAML2 Security Integration for SSO setup, and LOGIN POLICY to restrict authentication methods. Option A uses ALTER ACCOUNT SET with incorrect parameters. Option C uses invalid syntax. Option D describes an invalid session policy attribute.
questions.reference: Account Administration: SAML2 SSO and Login Policies

questions.stem: An administrator is setting up Snowflake for a new team and needs to create a proper role and object structure. The team needs a custom role DATASCIENCE_ROLE that will own a database DS_DB. Select TWO commands that correctly establish the intended ownership structure.

options.text: USE ROLE SYSADMIN; CREATE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; GRANT ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE TO ROLE SYSADMIN;
USE ROLE SYSADMIN; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB; GRANT OWNERSHIP ON DATABASE DS_DB TO ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE;
USE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB; (This creates the database owned by DATASCIENCE_ROLE)
USE ROLE SECURITYADMIN; CREATE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; GRANT ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE TO ROLE SYSADMIN;

questions.explanation: Creating the database while using DATASCIENCE_ROLE (option C) automatically sets DATASCIENCE_ROLE as the owner — the creating role becomes the owner in Snowflake. Creating the role under SECURITYADMIN (option D) and granting it to SYSADMIN follows best practice role management (SECURITYADMIN is the appropriate role for creating roles and users; SYSADMIN should not be used for role creation ideally). Option A creates the role using SYSADMIN — technically possible but SECURITYADMIN is more appropriate for role management. Option B (SYSADMIN creates the DB then grants ownership) also works but transfers ownership, which is a less direct approach and SYSADMIN would briefly own the DB.
questions.reference: Role Hierarchy: Creating Roles and Setting Object Ownership

questions.stem: A company needs to restrict the maximum duration that any individual SQL statement can run, regardless of the warehouse. Which account parameter controls this?

options.text: LOCK_TIMEOUT
QUERY_TAG
STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
SESSION_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS sets the maximum number of seconds a SQL statement can run before it is automatically cancelled. It can be set at the account, warehouse, or session level, with more specific levels overriding broader ones. LOCK_TIMEOUT controls how long a query waits to acquire a lock (not total execution time). QUERY_TAG is a label for tracking queries. SESSION_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS is not a standard Snowflake account parameter — idle session timeout is managed through session policies.
questions.reference: Account Parameters: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to set up a resource monitor that sends a notification email when 80% of the monthly quota is consumed, suspends the warehouse when 100% is reached, but does NOT want to create a new trigger for every 10% above 100%. Which trigger configuration is needed?

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 110 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO ALERT ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 110 PERCENT DO SUSPEND

questions.explanation: The requirement is exactly two triggers: NOTIFY at 80% and SUSPEND at 100%. SUSPEND allows running queries to finish before suspending the warehouse. There is no requirement for additional triggers above 100%. Option A adds an unnecessary SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 110%. ALERT (option C) is not a valid trigger action — the valid actions are NOTIFY, SUSPEND, and SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY. Option D has NOTIFY at 100% which delays the suspension to 110%, contradicting the requirement.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: Trigger Configuration

questions.stem: A company wants to analyze which virtual warehouses have consumed the most credits over the past 7 days, grouped by hour. Which query provides this information?

options.text: SELECT WAREHOUSE_NAME, DATE_TRUNC('HOUR', START_TIME) AS HOUR, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC;
SELECT SERVICE_TYPE, USAGE_DATE, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE USAGE_DATE >= DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_DATE()) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC;
SELECT WAREHOUSE_NAME, CREDITS_USED FROM SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD('hour', -168, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) ORDER BY CREDITS_USED DESC;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY(DATE_RANGE_START => DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_DATE())));

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY provides per-warehouse, per-hour credit consumption. The query groups by warehouse and hour (DATE_TRUNC to the hour) and sums credits over the past 7 days — exactly what is needed. Option B uses METERING_DAILY_HISTORY which is daily granularity, not hourly, and does not break down by warehouse. Option C (INFORMATION_SCHEMA) is the table-valued function form with limited retention (14 days) but the syntax is missing the DATE_RANGE_END parameter and the aggregation needed. Option D uses the INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function form which also works for 14-day lookbacks but does not aggregate by hour.
questions.reference: Account Usage: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to alert the finance team when daily credit consumption across all warehouses exceeds 500 credits. Which TWO approaches can provide this capability?

options.text: Create a resource monitor at the account level with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = DAILY, trigger action NOTIFY at 100%
Create a Snowflake Alert that queries METERING_DAILY_HISTORY and sends a notification when the daily total exceeds 500
Set ALTER ACCOUNT SET MAX_DAILY_CREDITS = 500 to automatically alert and stop compute when the limit is reached
Create a Snowflake Task that runs daily, queries WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY, and calls SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL if the threshold is exceeded

questions.explanation: A daily account-level resource monitor (option A) is a built-in Snowflake mechanism specifically for credit threshold notifications. Snowflake Alerts (option B) allow scheduling a condition check query against ACCOUNT_USAGE views and triggering notifications — this provides flexible, custom threshold monitoring. Snowflake Tasks + SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL (option D) would technically work as a custom solution but requires more setup; Snowflake Alerts are the more direct built-in feature for this. ALTER ACCOUNT SET MAX_DAILY_CREDITS (option C) is not a valid Snowflake parameter.
questions.reference: Resource Monitoring: Resource Monitors and Snowflake Alerts

questions.stem: An administrator notices that Snowpipe is generating unexpectedly high serverless credit charges. Resource monitors assigned to the production warehouses show healthy consumption. Why are the Snowpipe charges not captured in the warehouse-level resource monitors?

options.text: Snowpipe credits are counted toward whichever warehouse processed the most data that day
Snowpipe uses serverless compute that is separate from virtual warehouses; resource monitors only track virtual warehouse credits, not serverless credits
Snowpipe credits appear in resource monitors only after a 24-hour delay
Snowpipe requires a dedicated warehouse; the administrator needs to create a SNOWPIPE warehouse and assign the monitor to it

questions.explanation: Snowpipe is a serverless feature — it does not use customer-managed virtual warehouses. Instead, it uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute that is billed separately. Resource monitors only track credits consumed by virtual warehouses. Serverless credits (Snowpipe, automatic clustering, materialized view maintenance, search optimization, Snowpark Container Services) are not captured by resource monitors. To monitor these, administrators must use METERING_DAILY_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'SNOWPIPE', or use the BUDGET feature. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect — Snowpipe has its own serverless billing pathway.
questions.reference: Serverless Credits: Snowpipe vs Virtual Warehouse Billing

questions.stem: A resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 and FREQUENCY = MONTHLY is assigned to both the ANALYTICS_WH and REPORTING_WH. After 15 days, ANALYTICS_WH has consumed 700 credits and REPORTING_WH has consumed 200 credits. The NOTIFY trigger is set at 80%. What is the current status?

options.text: No notification triggered yet; each warehouse is evaluated independently (700/1000 = 70% for ANALYTICS_WH, 200/1000 = 20% for REPORTING_WH)
Notification triggered; the combined consumption is 900 credits (900/1000 = 90%), which exceeds the 80% threshold
Two separate notifications triggered, one for each warehouse as they are tracked independently
No notification triggered; resource monitors only count credits from the warehouse that consumes the most

questions.explanation: When a single resource monitor is assigned to multiple warehouses, credits consumed by ALL assigned warehouses are pooled and counted toward the single shared quota. ANALYTICS_WH (700) + REPORTING_WH (200) = 900 credits total. 900/1000 = 90%, which has already exceeded the 80% NOTIFY threshold. A notification email should have been sent. The warehouses are not tracked independently against separate quotas — they share the same 1000-credit pool. Option A incorrectly treats them independently. Option C implies separate tracking. Option D is incorrect.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: Shared Quota Across Multiple Warehouses

questions.stem: A new table is created by ROLE_A in the ANALYTICS schema. ROLE_B needs to query this table. What is the MINIMUM set of privileges ROLE_B requires?

options.text: SELECT on the table only
USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and SELECT on the table
USAGE on the database and SELECT on the table
OWNERSHIP on the table

questions.explanation: Snowflake requires a role to have USAGE at every level of the object hierarchy to access an object. To query a table: (1) USAGE on the database to access it, (2) USAGE on the schema to see into it, and (3) SELECT on the table to read its rows. Missing any one of these results in an 'object does not exist or access denied' error. SELECT alone (option A) is not sufficient without the container USAGEs. USAGE on database without schema USAGE (option C) is also insufficient. OWNERSHIP (option D) is far more than minimum necessary.
questions.reference: Access Control: Minimum Privileges for Table Access

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to grant a role the ability to create objects in a schema WITHOUT giving the role full ownership of the schema. Which privilege achieves this?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT CREATE TABLE ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT MODIFY ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;

questions.explanation: GRANT CREATE TABLE ON SCHEMA (or CREATE VIEW, CREATE STAGE, CREATE FUNCTION, etc.) grants the ability to create that specific type of object within the schema without granting schema ownership. Multiple CREATE privileges can be granted to give broader object creation capability. GRANT USAGE only allows navigating into the schema and accessing objects, not creating new ones. GRANT MODIFY on a schema changes schema-level properties, not object creation rights. GRANT OWNERSHIP transfers full control of the schema, which is excessive.
questions.reference: Schema Privileges: CREATE TABLE and Other CREATE Grants

questions.stem: A security team requires that all PII columns in the database are masked for users without the COMPLIANCE_ROLE. The masking policy should be applied to 50 tables across 5 schemas. Select TWO efficient approaches to apply the masking policy at scale.

options.text: Write a stored procedure that queries INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS for columns with a naming convention (e.g., ending in '_PII') and programmatically applies the masking policy using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN SET MASKING POLICY
Use a TAG-based masking policy: create a tag (e.g., PII_TAG), apply the tag to all PII columns, and attach the masking policy to the tag using ALTER TAG SET MASKING POLICY
Create one masking policy per table and apply each individually using separate ALTER TABLE statements for each column
Grant the masking policy at the schema level to automatically apply to all current and future columns

questions.explanation: The stored procedure approach (option A) allows programmatic, scalable application based on column naming conventions — one script applies policies across all tables. Tag-based masking (option B) is the most scalable approach: attach a tag like 'PII' to any column, and the masking policy automatically applies to all columns with that tag — including future columns when the tag is applied. Creating one policy per table (option C) is both tedious and does not scale. There is no schema-level masking policy grant that auto-applies to all columns (option D is not a valid Snowflake feature).
questions.reference: Dynamic Data Masking: Tag-Based Application at Scale

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator runs the following command: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE mydb TO ROLE reporting_role; A new table is created in mydb three days later. Does reporting_role have SELECT on the new table?

options.text: Yes, because the GRANT ON ALL TABLES grant applies to all current and future tables
No, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES is a one-time bulk grant to existing tables only; it does not create a future grant
Yes, but only if the new table is created by SYSADMIN or a role reporting_role can inherit from
No, because bulk grants at the database level are not supported in Snowflake

questions.explanation: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE is a bulk one-time grant that applies to all tables that exist at the time the command is run. It does NOT create a future grant. Tables created after this command will NOT automatically receive the SELECT grant for reporting_role. To handle future tables, a separate GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN DATABASE mydb TO ROLE reporting_role must also be executed. This is a common Snowflake gotcha — 'ALL TABLES' is retrospective only; 'FUTURE TABLES' is prospective.
questions.reference: Grants: ALL TABLES vs FUTURE TABLES

questions.stem: An administrator creates a row access policy that restricts data based on the user's assigned regions in a mapping table. During testing, ACCOUNTADMIN queries the table and sees no rows. Select TWO reasons this behavior is expected and what should be done.

options.text: Row access policies apply to all roles including ACCOUNTADMIN; ACCOUNTADMIN is not exempt
ACCOUNTADMIN's username is likely not in the region_mapping table, so the policy returns FALSE for all rows
The fix is to add an explicit check in the policy: CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' OR (mapping table condition)
The fix is to run ALTER TABLE ... ALTER ROW ACCESS POLICY BYPASS ROLES = ('ACCOUNTADMIN')

questions.explanation: Row access policies in Snowflake apply universally — including to ACCOUNTADMIN. There is no automatic bypass. The behavior seen (zero rows) is expected when ACCOUNTADMIN is not in the mapping table, because the policy evaluates to FALSE for every row. The correct fix (option C) is to add explicit privileged role bypass logic in the policy definition itself, e.g.: CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' THEN TRUE ELSE EXISTS(mapping table lookup) END. Option B is correct as a diagnosis but not listed as a fix action. ALTER TABLE ... BYPASS ROLES (option D) is not valid Snowflake syntax for row access policies.
questions.reference: Row Access Policies: Bypass Logic for Admin Roles

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to verify which network policy is currently applied to the account. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
SELECT CURRENT_NETWORK_POLICY();
DESCRIBE ACCOUNT;
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'NETWORK_POLICY' IN ACCOUNT;

questions.explanation: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'NETWORK_POLICY' IN ACCOUNT shows whether a network policy is set at the account level and which policy it is. This is the direct way to check the account-level network policy parameter. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all defined network policies but does not show which one is applied at the account level. SELECT CURRENT_NETWORK_POLICY() is not a valid Snowflake function. DESCRIBE ACCOUNT shows account metadata but not parameter settings.
questions.reference: Network Policies: Verifying Account-Level Assignment

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator notices that a network policy blocks connections from a specific IP that a vendor needs to use. The account policy is managed by a different team and cannot be changed quickly. The vendor's user account is VENDOR_USER. What is the fastest solution to allow the vendor access?

options.text: Create a new network policy that includes the vendor's IP and assign it to VENDOR_USER using ALTER USER VENDOR_USER SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy
Ask the vendor to use a VPN to access Snowflake from an approved IP range
Temporarily disable the account network policy using ALTER ACCOUNT UNSET NETWORK_POLICY
Add the vendor's IP to the ALLOWED_IP_LIST on the existing account network policy

questions.explanation: A user-level network policy overrides the account-level network policy for that specific user. Creating a vendor-specific policy that includes the vendor's IP and assigning it directly to VENDOR_USER is the fastest targeted solution that does not affect other users or require changing the account-level policy. Options B and D require coordination with other teams or the vendor. Option C removes protection for all users, which is a serious security risk.
questions.reference: Network Policies: User-Level Policy Override

questions.stem: A company using Azure is implementing Azure Private Link for Snowflake. Select TWO accurate statements about this implementation.

options.text: Azure Private Link for Snowflake requires Business Critical edition or higher
After enabling Private Link, the public internet endpoint for the Snowflake account is automatically disabled
Clients must update their connection strings to use the Private Link-specific hostname
Azure Private Link works across different Azure regions — the VNET and Snowflake account do not need to be in the same region

questions.explanation: Azure Private Link for Snowflake is only available on Business Critical edition (option A is true). Clients must use the Private Link-specific hostname (which includes 'privatelink' in the URL) — the standard public endpoint URL routes through the public internet, while the Private Link endpoint routes through Azure's private network backbone (option C is true). The public endpoint is NOT automatically disabled when Private Link is enabled — both can coexist; administrators must explicitly enforce Private Link-only access if needed (option B is false). Azure Private Link requires the VNET and the Snowflake account to be in the same Azure region (option D is false).
questions.reference: Private Connectivity: Azure Private Link Requirements

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is setting up external access integration for a Python UDF that needs to call an external REST API. The company's security policy requires that only specific external domains be allowed. What is the correct Snowflake construct to enforce this?

options.text: Create a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('<api_domain_ip>') and assign it to the UDF's owner role
Create a NETWORK RULE with TYPE = HOST and VALUE_LIST = ('api.example.com'), then include it in an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION
Set ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_DOMAINS = ('api.example.com')
Create a network policy with TYPE = EGRESS and add the domain to ALLOWED_DOMAINS

questions.explanation: For controlling egress from Snowflake UDFs and stored procedures to external services, the correct approach uses Network Rules (TYPE = HOST for domain-based rules) combined with External Access Integrations. A NETWORK RULE with TYPE = HOST specifies allowed external hostnames. The EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION object ties together the network rule and optional secrets, and is then referenced in the UDF/stored procedure definition. Network policies (options A and D) control inbound access to Snowflake, not outbound egress from UDFs. ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_DOMAINS (option C) is not a valid Snowflake parameter.
questions.reference: External Access Integrations: Network Rules for UDF Egress

questions.stem: A warehouse is configured with SIZE = 'X-LARGE', AUTO_SUSPEND = 300, and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. If the warehouse has been idle for 6 minutes (360 seconds), what is its current state?

options.text: Running but idle, because auto-suspend has not yet triggered
Suspended, because 360 seconds of idle time exceeds the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold of 300 seconds
Running, because X-LARGE warehouses have a minimum runtime of 10 minutes before auto-suspend
Suspended, but it will automatically resume every 30 minutes to check for queued queries

questions.explanation: AUTO_SUSPEND = 300 means the warehouse suspends after 300 seconds (5 minutes) of inactivity. With 360 seconds (6 minutes) of idle time, the threshold has been exceeded and the warehouse is suspended. Warehouse size (X-LARGE) does not affect the auto-suspend timing — it operates the same way regardless of size. Suspended warehouses do not periodically wake up to check for queries — they remain suspended until a query is submitted (with AUTO_RESUME = TRUE).
questions.reference: Warehouse Configuration: AUTO_SUSPEND Behavior

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to enable the Query Acceleration Service for a specific warehouse to help with ad-hoc analytical queries that occasionally scan very large datasets. The service should not scale beyond 4x the warehouse's compute for any single query. Which command configures this?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4;
ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET QUERY_ACCELERATION = ENABLED MAX_SCALE = 4;
CREATE QUERY ACCELERATION POLICY qa_policy MAX_SCALE = 4; ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET QUERY_ACCELERATION_POLICY = qa_policy;
ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE; ALTER ACCOUNT SET QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4;

questions.explanation: The correct Snowflake syntax to enable Query Acceleration Service uses ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE and optionally QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR to cap the scale factor (how much additional serverless compute can be added relative to the warehouse size). The default MAX_SCALE_FACTOR is 8 if not specified. Both parameters can be set in a single ALTER WAREHOUSE statement. Option B uses incorrect parameter names. Option C invents a non-existent QUERY ACCELERATION POLICY object. Option D incorrectly tries to set the scale factor at the account level rather than the warehouse level.
questions.reference: Query Acceleration Service: Configuration Parameters

questions.stem: A DBA is evaluating the storage costs for a Snowflake account on Enterprise edition. Select TWO true statements about how storage is billed in Snowflake.

options.text: Time Travel storage (historical versions of modified or deleted data) is billed at the same rate as active storage
Fail-Safe storage (the 7-day Snowflake-managed protection period) is charged to the customer in addition to Time Travel storage
Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on a table immediately frees up Time Travel storage and reduces costs
Cloning a table creates an independent copy of all data immediately, doubling the storage cost for the cloned table

questions.explanation: Time Travel storage is billed at the same per-TB rate as active storage (option A is true). Fail-Safe provides an additional 7 days of data protection managed by Snowflake and is also billed to the customer (option B is true) — total protected storage can be active + Time Travel + Fail-Safe. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS does not immediately purge historical versions — Snowflake's background processes eventually clean up, but it is not instant (option C is false). Table cloning in Snowflake uses zero-copy cloning — no data is physically copied at clone time, so storage cost is not doubled immediately; only divergent (changed) data incurs additional storage (option D is false).
questions.reference: Storage Billing: Active, Time Travel, and Fail-Safe Storage Costs

questions.stem: An administrator is enabling automatic clustering on a very large table (10 TB) with a cluster key of (REGION, ORDER_DATE). The table receives heavy INSERT activity throughout the day. What ongoing cost consideration should the administrator communicate to stakeholders?

options.text: Automatic clustering is free because it uses idle warehouse compute cycles
Automatic clustering consumes serverless credits continuously as Snowflake reclusters micro-partitions in the background, charged separately from warehouse credits
Automatic clustering charges a one-time fee at the time the CLUSTER BY key is defined
Automatic clustering uses the warehouse's existing compute and is included in the warehouse's credit consumption

questions.explanation: Automatic clustering is a serverless feature — it uses Snowflake-managed background compute, not the customer's virtual warehouses. Credits are charged continuously as the service reclusters micro-partitions. For a large, heavily-modified table, these charges can be significant. Administrators should monitor ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY to track credit consumption. There is no one-time fee. It is not free, and it does not use existing warehouse compute (it is completely separate from virtual warehouse billing).
questions.reference: Automatic Clustering: Serverless Credit Billing

questions.stem: A CISO requests a report of all queries that accessed the PATIENTS table containing PHI data in the last 6 months, including the user, timestamp, and specific columns accessed. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and approach best satisfies this request?

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TEXT ILIKE '%PATIENTS%' to find queries referencing the table
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY WHERE DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED LIKE '%PATIENTS%' to get structured access records with column-level detail
Query SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY for the past 6 months of query logs
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY to see all users who logged in during that period

questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY is the correct view for PHI access auditing. It contains structured records of which objects (tables, views) and columns were accessed by each query, captured by user, role, timestamp, and query ID. DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED and BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED are semi-structured columns (arrays of objects) that contain table and column names. This provides the precise column-level audit trail needed for PHI compliance. QUERY_HISTORY text search (option A) is unreliable — queries might use aliases, views, or dynamic SQL. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY only has 7-day retention (option C). LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication, not data access (option D).
questions.reference: Audit & Compliance: ACCESS_HISTORY for PHI Auditing

questions.stem: A security team needs to identify all privilege grants that occurred in the past 30 days, including who granted them and to whom. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not retain sufficient history. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and columns provide this information?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES — columns CREATED_ON, PRIVILEGE, GRANTED_ON, NAME, GRANTEE_NAME, GRANTED_BY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_USERS — columns CREATED_ON, ROLE, GRANTEE_NAME, GRANTED_BY
Both GRANTS_TO_ROLES and GRANTS_TO_USERS should be queried to capture all privilege grant activity
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered for QUERY_TYPE = 'GRANT'

questions.explanation: Privilege grants in Snowflake are of two types: privileges granted to roles (object privileges like SELECT, INSERT, USAGE — captured in GRANTS_TO_ROLES) and role grants to users (GRANTS_TO_USERS). To capture ALL grant activity from the past 30 days, both views must be queried. GRANTS_TO_ROLES captures when privileges on objects were granted to roles (including GRANTED_BY to see who issued the grant). GRANTS_TO_USERS captures when roles were granted to users. Both views have 1-year retention. QUERY_HISTORY with QUERY_TYPE = 'GRANT' could work but provides raw SQL without structured privilege metadata.
questions.reference: Account Usage: GRANTS_TO_ROLES and GRANTS_TO_USERS

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is configuring data masking for a financial application. The CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER column should show the full number to FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE and a masked version (showing only last 4 digits) to all other roles. Select TWO valid ways to implement this conditional masking logic in the policy.

options.text: CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE' THEN val ELSE CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)) END
IFF(IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE'), val, CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)))
CASE WHEN CURRENT_USER() IN (SELECT username FROM fraud_analyst_users) THEN val ELSE 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX' END
GRANT UNMASK ON COLUMN credit_card_number TO ROLE FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE — no masking policy needed

questions.explanation: Option A uses CURRENT_ROLE() to check the active primary role and applies conditional masking. This is the standard pattern for role-based masking. Option B uses IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() which checks if a role is active in the session (including secondary roles), making it broader and useful when users may have the role as either primary or secondary. Both are valid masking policy expressions. Option C uses a user-based lookup which could work functionally but is not a recommended pattern (user names can change, and this mixes user identity with role-based access control). GRANT UNMASK (option D) is not a valid Snowflake privilege — masking is controlled entirely through policy logic.
questions.reference: Dynamic Data Masking: Conditional Role-Based Logic

questions.stem: A regulated financial services company requires that Snowflake activity logs be retained for 7 years for compliance purposes, but ACCOUNT_USAGE only retains data for 1 year. What architectural approach should the administrator implement?

options.text: Contact Snowflake support to extend ACCOUNT_USAGE retention to 7 years for an additional fee
Create Snowflake Tasks that periodically query ACCOUNT_USAGE views and INSERT results into customer-managed tables (compliance archive tables) with no time limit
Enable the Data Retention Extension feature in account settings to extend ACCOUNT_USAGE to 7 years
Use Snowflake's built-in compliance mode which automatically retains all audit logs for 7 years

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE views have a fixed 1-year retention that cannot be extended — Snowflake does not offer extended retention for these system views regardless of support requests (option A is false). The standard architectural pattern for long-term audit log retention is to use Snowflake Tasks to regularly extract ACCOUNT_USAGE data (LOGIN_HISTORY, QUERY_HISTORY, ACCESS_HISTORY, GRANTS_TO_ROLES, etc.) and insert it into customer-managed tables within the account (or export to external storage). Customer-managed tables have configurable retention. There is no Data Retention Extension feature for ACCOUNT_USAGE (option C is false). Snowflake does not have a built-in 7-year compliance mode (option D is false).
questions.reference: Audit & Compliance: Long-Term Log Retention Architecture
